title: Temporal Pooling Strategies for Training-Free Anomalous Sound Detection with Self-Supervised Audio Embeddings
summary: Training-free anomalous sound detection (ASD) based on pre-trained audio embedding models has recently garnered significant attention, as it enables the detection of anomalous sounds using only normal reference data while offering improved robustness under domain shifts. However, existing embedding-based approaches almost exclusively rely on temporal mean pooling, while alternative pooling strategies have so far only been explored for spectrogram-based representations. Consequently, the role of temporal pooling in training-free ASD with pre-trained embeddings remains insufficiently understood. In this paper, we present a systematic evaluation of temporal pooling strategies across multiple state-of-the-art audio embedding models. We propose relative deviation pooling (RDP), an adaptive pooling method that emphasizes informative temporal deviations, and introduce a hybrid pooling strategy that combines RDP with generalized mean pooling. Experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed methods consistently outperform mean pooling and achieve state-of-the-art performance for training-free ASD, including results that surpass all previously reported trained systems and ensembles on the DCASE2025 ASD dataset.

AI.tldr: Systematic evaluation of temporal pooling strategies for training-free anomalous sound detection using pre-trained audio embeddings, introducing relative deviation pooling and hybrid pooling that outperform mean pooling and achieve state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing embedding-based anomalous sound detection approaches rely almost exclusively on temporal mean pooling, while alternative pooling strategies have only been explored for spectrogram-based representations, leaving the role of temporal pooling in training-free ASD with pre-trained embeddings insufficiently understood.
AI.method: Proposes relative deviation pooling (RDP), an adaptive pooling method that emphasizes informative temporal deviations, and introduces a hybrid pooling strategy that combines RDP with generalized mean pooling. Conducts systematic evaluation of temporal pooling strategies across multiple state-of-the-art audio embedding models.
AI.result: Experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed methods consistently outperform mean pooling and achieve state-of-the-art performance for training-free ASD, including results that surpass all previously reported trained systems and ensembles on the DCASE2025 ASD dataset.
AI.conclusion: The systematic evaluation reveals the importance of temporal pooling strategies in training-free ASD, with the proposed relative deviation pooling and hybrid pooling methods offering superior performance over conventional mean pooling, establishing new state-of-the-art results for training-free anomalous sound detection.

title: An Approach to Simultaneous Acquisition of Real-Time MRI Video, EEG, and Surface EMG for Articulatory, Brain, and Muscle Activity During Speech Production
summary: Speech production is a complex process spanning neural planning, motor control, muscle activation, and articulatory kinematics. While the acoustic speech signal is the most accessible product of the speech production act, it does not directly reveal its causal neurophysiological substrates. We present the first simultaneous acquisition of real-time (dynamic) MRI, EEG, and surface EMG, capturing several key aspects of the speech production chain: brain signals, muscle activations, and articulatory movements. This multimodal acquisition paradigm presents substantial technical challenges, including MRI-induced electromagnetic interference and myogenic artifacts. To mitigate these, we introduce an artifact suppression pipeline tailored to this tri-modal setting. Once fully developed, this framework is poised to offer an unprecedented window into speech neuroscience and insights leading to brain-computer interface advances.

AI.tldr: First simultaneous acquisition of real-time MRI, EEG, and surface EMG for speech production research, with artifact suppression pipeline for tri-modal data.
AI.motivation: Speech production involves complex neural planning, motor control, and articulatory processes, but acoustic signals alone don't reveal causal neurophysiological substrates. Current methods lack simultaneous capture of brain signals, muscle activations, and articulatory movements.
AI.method: Developed first simultaneous acquisition of real-time (dynamic) MRI, EEG, and surface EMG. Created artifact suppression pipeline to mitigate MRI-induced electromagnetic interference and myogenic artifacts in this tri-modal setting.
AI.result: Successfully captured several key aspects of speech production chain simultaneously: brain signals (EEG), muscle activations (EMG), and articulatory movements (MRI). The artifact suppression pipeline addresses technical challenges of multimodal acquisition.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides unprecedented window into speech neuroscience and offers insights for brain-computer interface advances. Once fully developed, it will enable comprehensive study of speech production neurophysiology.

title: Voice Timbre Attribute Detection with Compact and Interpretable Training-Free Acoustic Parameters
summary: Voice timbre attribute detection (vTAD) is the task of determining the relative intensity of timbre attributes between speech utterances. Voice timbre is a crucial yet inherently complex component of speech perception. While deep neural network (DNN) embeddings perform well in speaker modelling, they often act as black-box representations with limited physical interpretability and high computational cost. In this work, a compact acoustic parameter set is investigated for vTAD. The set captures important acoustic measures and their temporal dynamics which are found to be crucial in the task. Despite its simplicity, the acoustic parameter set is competitive, outperforming conventional cepstral features and supervised DNN embeddings, and approaching state-of-the-art self-supervised models. Importantly, the studied set require no trainable parameters, incur negligible computation, and offer explicit interpretability for analysing physical traits behind human timbre perception.

AI.tldr: Compact acoustic parameter set for voice timbre attribute detection outperforms cepstral features and supervised DNN embeddings, approaches self-supervised models, with zero trainable parameters and high interpretability.
AI.motivation: Voice timbre attribute detection is important but complex; DNN embeddings work well but lack interpretability and have high computational cost. Need for compact, interpretable acoustic parameters.
AI.method: Investigated a compact acoustic parameter set capturing important acoustic measures and their temporal dynamics for voice timbre attribute detection. The set requires no trainable parameters.
AI.result: The acoustic parameter set is competitive, outperforming conventional cepstral features and supervised DNN embeddings, and approaching state-of-the-art self-supervised models.
AI.conclusion: Compact acoustic parameter set offers effective vTAD with negligible computation, zero trainable parameters, and explicit interpretability for analyzing physical traits behind human timbre perception.

title: PolyBench: A Benchmark for Compositional Reasoning in Polyphonic Audio
summary: Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) are increasingly capable of reasoning over audio. However, existing benchmarks provide limited coverage of reasoning in polyphonic audio, where multiple sound events co-occur and induce compositional structure. In this work, we introduce PolyBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate compositional reasoning in polyphonic audio. PolyBench comprises five evaluation subsets covering counting, classification, detection, concurrency, and duration estimation, requiring reasoning over multiple concurrent events and their relations. Evaluation of state-of-the-art LALMs reveals consistent performance degradation in polyphonic audio, indicating a fundamental bottleneck in current LALMs.

AI.tldr: PolyBench is a new benchmark for evaluating compositional reasoning in polyphonic audio, revealing performance degradation in current Large Audio Language Models when handling multiple concurrent sound events.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks provide limited coverage of reasoning in polyphonic audio where multiple sound events co-occur and create compositional structure, creating a gap in evaluating LALMs' ability to handle complex audio scenarios.
AI.method: Introduces PolyBench, a benchmark comprising five evaluation subsets: counting, classification, detection, concurrency, and duration estimation, designed to test reasoning over multiple concurrent events and their relations.
AI.result: Evaluation of state-of-the-art LALMs shows consistent performance degradation in polyphonic audio, indicating a fundamental bottleneck in current models' ability to handle compositional reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Current LALMs have significant limitations in compositional reasoning for polyphonic audio, and PolyBench provides a necessary benchmark to drive improvements in this critical area of audio understanding.

title: Risk-Aware Rulebooks for Multi-Objective Trajectory Evaluation under Uncertainty
summary: We present a risk-aware formalism for evaluating system trajectories in the presence of uncertain interactions between the system and its environment. The proposed formalism supports reasoning under uncertainty and systematically handles complex relationships among requirements and objectives, including hierarchical priorities and non-comparability. Rather than treating the environment as exogenous noise, we explicitly model how each system trajectory influences the environment and evaluate trajectories under the resulting distribution of environment responses. We prove that the formalism induces a preorder on the set of system trajectories, ensuring consistency and preventing cyclic preferences. Finally, we illustrate the approach with an autonomous driving example that demonstrates how the formalism enhances explainability by clarifying the rationale behind trajectory selection.

AI.tldr: Risk-aware trajectory evaluation framework that models system-environment interactions as uncertain influences rather than exogenous noise, ensuring consistent preferences and enhancing explainability.
AI.motivation: Existing trajectory evaluation methods often treat environmental interactions as exogenous noise, failing to capture how system trajectories actively influence environment responses and handle complex relationships among requirements with hierarchical priorities and non-comparability.
AI.method: Proposes a risk-aware formalism that explicitly models how each system trajectory influences the environment, evaluates trajectories under resulting distributions of environment responses, supports reasoning under uncertainty, and systematically handles complex requirement relationships including hierarchical priorities and non-comparability.
AI.result: Proves the formalism induces a preorder on system trajectories, ensuring consistency and preventing cyclic preferences. Demonstrates enhanced explainability through an autonomous driving example that clarifies rationale behind trajectory selection.
AI.conclusion: The risk-aware formalism provides a principled approach for trajectory evaluation that captures bidirectional system-environment interactions, ensures preference consistency, and improves decision transparency in complex, uncertain environments.

title: BabAR: from phoneme recognition to developmental measures of young children's speech production
summary: Studying early speech development at scale requires automatic tools, yet automatic phoneme recognition, especially for young children, remains largely unsolved. Building on decades of data collection, we curate TinyVox, a corpus of more than half a million phonetically transcribed child vocalizations in English, French, Portuguese, German, and Spanish. We use TinyVox to train BabAR, a cross-linguistic phoneme recognition system for child speech. We find that pretraining the system on multilingual child-centered daylong recordings substantially outperforms alternatives, and that providing 20 seconds of surrounding audio context during fine-tuning further improves performance. Error analyses show that substitutions predominantly fall within the same broad phonetic categories, suggesting suitability for coarse-grained developmental analyses. We validate BabAR by showing that its automatic measures of speech maturity align with developmental estimates from the literature.

AI.tldr: BabAR is a cross-linguistic phoneme recognition system for child speech trained on TinyVox corpus of 500k+ child vocalizations across 5 languages, using multilingual pretraining and audio context to achieve developmental analysis suitability.
AI.motivation: Automatic phoneme recognition for young children remains largely unsolved, hindering large-scale study of early speech development. Existing tools are insufficient for analyzing child speech across multiple languages.
AI.method: Curated TinyVox corpus of 500k+ phonetically transcribed child vocalizations in English, French, Portuguese, German, and Spanish. Trained BabAR system using multilingual pretraining on child-centered daylong recordings and fine-tuning with 20 seconds of surrounding audio context.
AI.result: Multilingual pretraining substantially outperforms alternatives, and audio context during fine-tuning further improves performance. Error analyses show substitutions fall within same broad phonetic categories, making it suitable for coarse-grained developmental analyses. Automatic measures align with developmental estimates from literature.
AI.conclusion: BabAR provides an effective automatic phoneme recognition system for child speech across multiple languages, enabling large-scale developmental studies of early speech acquisition with validated developmental alignment.

title: Joint Visible Light and RF Backscatter Communications for Ambient IoT Network: Fundamentals, Applications, and Opportunities
summary: The rapid growth of the Internet of Things (IoT) devices in the sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks raises significant generality and scalability challenges due to energy consumption, deployment complexity, and environmental impact. Ambient IoT (A-IoT), leveraging ambient energy harvesting (EH) for batteryless device operation, has emerged as a promising solution to address these challenges.Among various EH and communication techniques, visible light communication (VLC) integrated with ambient backscatter communication (AmBC) offers remarkable advantages, including energy neutrality, high reliability, and enhanced security. In this paper, we propose a joint VLC-AmBC architecture, emphasizing fundamental concepts, system designs, and practical implementations. We explore potential applications in environmental monitoring, healthcare, smart logistics, and secure communications. We present proof-of-concept demonstrations for three distinct types of ambient backscatter devices (AmBDs): EH-Only, VLC-Relay, and VLC-Control. Experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of implementing joint VLC-AmBC systems, highlighting their practical viability across various deployment scenarios. Finally, we outline future research directions, including integrated sensing and communication, as well as optimized energy-efficient deployment. Open issues, such as large-scale deployment challenges, are also discussed, thereby providing a clear roadmap for future developments in joint VLC-AmBC-enabled A-IoT ecosystems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a joint Visible Light Communication-Ambient Backscatter Communication (VLC-AmBC) architecture for batteryless Ambient IoT devices, demonstrating feasibility through proof-of-concept implementations and outlining future research directions.
AI.motivation: Addresses energy consumption, deployment complexity, and environmental impact challenges in 6G IoT networks by leveraging ambient energy harvesting for batteryless operation through VLC-AmBC integration.
AI.method: Proposes a joint VLC-AmBC architecture with fundamental concepts, system designs, and practical implementations. Demonstrates three types of ambient backscatter devices: EH-Only, VLC-Relay, and VLC-Control through proof-of-concept experiments.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of implementing joint VLC-AmBC systems, showing practical viability across various deployment scenarios including environmental monitoring, healthcare, smart logistics, and secure communications.
AI.conclusion: Joint VLC-AmBC offers a promising solution for batteryless Ambient IoT with energy neutrality, reliability, and security advantages. Future directions include integrated sensing and communication, optimized energy-efficient deployment, and addressing large-scale deployment challenges.

title: Visual-Informed Speech Enhancement Using Attention-Based Beamforming
summary: Recent studies have demonstrated that incorporating auxiliary information, such as speaker voiceprint or visual cues, can substantially improve Speech Enhancement (SE) performance. However, single-channel methods often yield suboptimal results in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions, when there is high reverberation, or in complex scenarios involving dynamic speakers, overlapping speech, or non-stationary noise. To address these issues, we propose a novel Visual-Informed Neural Beamforming Network (VI-NBFNet), which integrates microphone array signal processing and deep neural networks (DNNs) using multimodal input features. The proposed network leverages a pretrained visual speech recognition model to extract lip movements as input features, which serve for voice activity detection (VAD) and target speaker identification. The system is intended to handle both static and moving speakers by introducing a supervised end-to-end beamforming framework equipped with an attention mechanism. The experimental results demonstrated that the proposed audiovisual system has achieved better SE performance and robustness for both stationary and dynamic speaker scenarios, compared to several baseline methods.

AI.tldr: VI-NBFNet: A visual-informed neural beamforming network that integrates microphone array processing with deep neural networks using lip movement features from pretrained visual speech recognition models to improve speech enhancement in challenging scenarios.
AI.motivation: Single-channel speech enhancement methods perform poorly in low SNR conditions, high reverberation, and complex scenarios with dynamic speakers, overlapping speech, or non-stationary noise. Existing methods lack robustness in handling both static and moving speakers effectively.
AI.method: Proposes VI-NBFNet that integrates microphone array signal processing with DNNs using multimodal features. Uses pretrained visual speech recognition model to extract lip movements for voice activity detection and target speaker identification. Implements supervised end-to-end beamforming framework with attention mechanism to handle both static and moving speakers.
AI.result: The audiovisual system achieved better speech enhancement performance and robustness for both stationary and dynamic speaker scenarios compared to several baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: VI-NBFNet effectively addresses limitations of single-channel methods by incorporating visual cues and array processing, demonstrating superior performance in challenging acoustic environments with both static and dynamic speakers.

title: On boundedness of solutions of three-state Moore-Greitzer compressor model with nonlinear proportional-integral controller for the surge subsystem
summary: The work focuses on Lagrange stability of the origin for the three-state Moore-Greitzer compressor model in closed loop with a nonlinear PI controller, tuned only to stabilize a lower-dimensional invariant surge-dynamics subsystem.The linearization of the system is not stabilizable but the static nonlinearity satisfies a sector condition, and together with a structural property of the stall-dynamics subsystem, this plays an essential role in the analysis. The main contribution provides explicit conditions on the controller parameters together with analytical arguments that guarantee boundedness of all solutions of the closed-loop system. The analysis employs a non-standard application of circle-criterion-based arguments. Together with the additional arguments developed in the work, this stability test also shows that the closed-loop system is robust to certain perturbations and model uncertainties.

AI.tldr: Analysis of Lagrange stability for a 3-state Moore-Greitzer compressor model with nonlinear PI controller using circle-criterion arguments to guarantee boundedness of all solutions.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of stabilizing the origin of the three-state Moore-Greitzer compressor model when the linearization is not stabilizable, requiring nonlinear control techniques to ensure boundedness of all solutions.
AI.method: Uses a nonlinear PI controller tuned to stabilize only the lower-dimensional invariant surge-dynamics subsystem. Employs non-standard application of circle-criterion-based arguments combined with analysis of sector conditions and structural properties of the stall-dynamics subsystem.
AI.result: Provides explicit conditions on controller parameters and analytical arguments that guarantee boundedness of all solutions (Lagrange stability). The stability test also demonstrates robustness to certain perturbations and model uncertainties.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully establishes Lagrange stability for the compressor model despite non-stabilizable linearization, using nonlinear control and circle-criterion arguments to handle the system's structural properties and sector-bounded nonlinearities.

title: When Denoising Hinders: Revisiting Zero-Shot ASR with SAM-Audio and Whisper
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. IEEE Conference Paper
summary: Recent advances in automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech enhancement have led to a widespread assumption that improving perceptual audio quality should directly benefit recognition accuracy. In this work, we rigorously examine whether this assumption holds for modern zero-shot ASR systems. We present a systematic empirical study on the impact of Segment Anything Model Audio by Meta AI, a recent foundation-scale speech enhancement model proposed by Meta, when used as a preprocessing step for zero-shot transcription with Whisper. Experiments are conducted across multiple Whisper model variants and two linguistically distinct noisy speech datasets: a real-world Bengali YouTube corpus and a publicly available English noisy dataset. Contrary to common intuition, our results show that SAM-Audio preprocessing consistently degrades ASR performance, increasing both Word Error Rate (WER) and Character Error Rate (CER) compared to raw noisy speech, despite substantial improvements in signal-level quality. Objective Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio analysis on the English dataset confirms that SAM-Audio produces acoustically cleaner signals, yet this improvement fails to translate into recognition gains. Therefore, we conducted a detailed utterance-level analysis to understand this counterintuitive result. We found that the recognition degradation is a systematic issue affecting the majority of the audio, not just isolated outliers, and that the errors worsen as the Whisper model size increases. These findings expose a fundamental mismatch: audio that is perceptually cleaner to human listeners is not necessarily robust for machine recognition. This highlights the risk of blindly applying state-of-the-art denoising as a preprocessing step in zero-shot ASR pipelines.

AI.tldr: SAM-Audio speech enhancement preprocessing consistently degrades zero-shot ASR performance with Whisper despite improving audio quality, revealing a mismatch between perceptual cleanliness and machine recognition robustness.
AI.motivation: To rigorously test the common assumption that improving perceptual audio quality should directly benefit ASR accuracy, specifically examining whether modern speech enhancement models like SAM-Audio actually improve zero-shot transcription performance with Whisper.
AI.method: Systematic empirical study using SAM-Audio as preprocessing for zero-shot transcription with Whisper across multiple model variants and two linguistically distinct noisy speech datasets: a real-world Bengali YouTube corpus and a publicly available English noisy dataset, with objective PSNR analysis and detailed utterance-level error analysis.
AI.result: Contrary to expectations, SAM-Audio preprocessing consistently degrades ASR performance, increasing both WER and CER compared to raw noisy speech despite substantial signal-level quality improvements. Recognition degradation is systematic across most audio, worsens with larger Whisper models, and occurs even when PSNR confirms acoustically cleaner signals.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental mismatch between perceptual audio quality and machine recognition robustness; acoustically cleaner signals that sound better to humans do not necessarily improve ASR performance, highlighting the risk of blindly applying state-of-the-art denoising as preprocessing in zero-shot ASR pipelines.

title: Hierarchical Decoding for Discrete Speech Synthesis with Multi-Resolution Spoof Detection
summary: Neural codec language models enable high-quality discrete speech synthesis, yet their inference remains vulnerable to token-level artifacts and distributional drift that degrade perceptual realism. Rather than relying on preference optimization or retraining, we propose MSpoof-TTS, a training-free inference framework that improves zero-shot synthesis through multi-resolution spoof guidance. We introduce a Multi-Resolution Token-based Spoof Detection framework that evaluates codec sequences at different temporal granularities to detect locally inconsistent or unnatural patterns. We then integrate the spoof detectors into a hierarchical decoding strategy, progressively pruning low-quality candidates and re-ranking hypotheses. This discriminator-guided generation enhances robustness without modifying model parameters. Experiments validate the effectiveness of our framework for robust and high-quality codec-based speech generation.

AI.tldr: MSpoof-TTS is a training-free inference framework that uses multi-resolution spoof detection to improve zero-shot speech synthesis quality in neural codec language models by detecting and pruning low-quality token sequences.
AI.motivation: Neural codec language models produce high-quality discrete speech synthesis but suffer from token-level artifacts and distributional drift during inference that degrade perceptual realism. Existing solutions require preference optimization or retraining, which are computationally expensive.
AI.method: Proposes MSpoof-TTS: a training-free inference framework with Multi-Resolution Token-based Spoof Detection that evaluates codec sequences at different temporal granularities to detect locally inconsistent or unnatural patterns. Integrates spoof detectors into hierarchical decoding strategy that progressively prunes low-quality candidates and re-ranks hypotheses without modifying model parameters.
AI.result: Experiments validate the effectiveness of the framework for robust and high-quality codec-based speech generation. The discriminator-guided generation enhances robustness without requiring model parameter modifications.
AI.conclusion: MSpoof-TTS provides an effective training-free solution to improve zero-shot synthesis quality in neural codec language models by addressing token-level artifacts and distributional drift through multi-resolution spoof guidance and hierarchical decoding.

title: The Vertical Challenge of Low-Altitude Economy: Why We Need a Unified Height System?
summary: The explosive growth of the low-altitude economy, driven by eVTOLs and UAVs, demands a unified digital infrastructure to ensure safety and scalability. However, the current aviation vertical references are dangerously fragmented: manned aviation relies on barometric pressure, cartography uses Mean Sea Level (MSL), and obstacle avoidance depends on Above Ground Level (AGL). This fragmentation creates significant ambiguity for autonomous systems and hinders cross-stakeholder interoperability. In this article, we propose Height Above Ellipsoid (HAE) as the standardized vertical reference for lower airspace. Unlike legacy systems prone to environmental drift and inconsistent datums, HAE provides a globally consistent, GNSS-native, and mathematically stable reference. We present a pragmatic bidirectional transformation framework to bridge HAE with legacy systems and demonstrate its efficacy through (1) real-world implementation in Shenzhen's partitioned airspace management, and (2) a probabilistic risk assessment driven by empirical flight logs from the PX4 ecosystem. Results show that transitioning to HAE reduces the required vertical separation minimum, effectively increasing dynamic airspace capacity while maintaining a target safety level. This work offers a roadmap for transitioning from analog height keeping to a digital-native vertical standard.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Height Above Ellipsoid (HAE) as a standardized vertical reference for low-altitude airspace to replace fragmented legacy systems, demonstrating increased airspace capacity while maintaining safety through real-world implementation and risk assessment.
AI.motivation: The explosive growth of low-altitude economy (eVTOLs/UAVs) requires unified digital infrastructure, but current aviation vertical references are dangerously fragmented: manned aviation uses barometric pressure, cartography uses Mean Sea Level (MSL), and obstacle avoidance uses Above Ground Level (AGL). This fragmentation creates ambiguity for autonomous systems and hinders cross-stakeholder interoperability.
AI.method: Proposes Height Above Ellipsoid (HAE) as standardized vertical reference for lower airspace, providing globally consistent, GNSS-native, mathematically stable reference. Presents pragmatic bidirectional transformation framework to bridge HAE with legacy systems. Demonstrates efficacy through: (1) real-world implementation in Shenzhen's partitioned airspace management, and (2) probabilistic risk assessment driven by empirical flight logs from PX4 ecosystem.
AI.result: Transitioning to HAE reduces required vertical separation minimum, effectively increasing dynamic airspace capacity while maintaining target safety level. The framework enables practical transition from analog height keeping to digital-native vertical standard.
AI.conclusion: HAE offers a roadmap for transitioning from analog height keeping to a digital-native vertical standard, addressing fragmentation issues in low-altitude airspace management and enabling safer, more scalable operations for the growing low-altitude economy.

title: WhisperAlign: Word-Boundary-Aware ASR and WhisperX-Anchored Pyannote Diarization for Long-Form Bengali Speech
summary: This paper presents our solution for the DL Sprint 4.0, addressing the dual challenges of Bengali Long-Form Speech Recognition (Task 1) and Speaker Diarization (Task 2). Processing long-form, multi-speaker Bengali audio introduces significant hurdles in voice activity detection, overlapping speech, and context preservation. To solve the long-form transcription challenge, we implemented a robust audio chunking strategy utilizing whisper-timestamped, allowing us to feed precise, context-aware segments into our fine-tuned acoustic model for high-accuracy transcription. For the diarization task, we developed an integrated pipeline leveraging pyannote.audio and WhisperX. A key contribution of our approach is the domain-specific fine-tuning of the Pyannote segmentation model on the competition dataset. This adaptation allowed the model to better capture the nuances of Bengali conversational dynamics and accurately resolve complex, overlapping speaker boundaries. Our methodology demonstrates that applying intelligent timestamped chunking to ASR and targeted segmentation fine-tuning to diarization significantly drives down Word Error Rate (WER) and Diarization Error Rate (DER), in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a solution for DL Sprint 4.0 addressing Bengali long-form speech recognition and speaker diarization using whisper-timestamped chunking for ASR and domain-specific fine-tuning of Pyannote segmentation for diarization.
AI.motivation: Processing long-form, multi-speaker Bengali audio presents challenges in voice activity detection, overlapping speech, and context preservation in low-resource settings.
AI.method: 1) For ASR: Robust audio chunking using whisper-timestamped to create precise, context-aware segments fed into fine-tuned acoustic model. 2) For diarization: Integrated pipeline using pyannote.audio and WhisperX with domain-specific fine-tuning of Pyannote segmentation model on competition dataset.
AI.result: The approach significantly reduces Word Error Rate (WER) and Diarization Error Rate (DER) by applying intelligent timestamped chunking for ASR and targeted segmentation fine-tuning for diarization.
AI.conclusion: Intelligent timestamped chunking for ASR combined with domain-specific fine-tuning of segmentation models effectively addresses Bengali long-form speech recognition and speaker diarization challenges in low-resource settings.

title: Design of Grid Forming Multi Timescale Coordinated Control Strategies for Dynamic Virtual Power Plants
summary: As the penetration level of distributed energy resources (DERs) continues to rise, traditional frequency and voltage support from synchronous machines declines. This weakens grid stability and increases the need for fast and adaptive control in a dynamic manner, especially in weak grids. However, most virtual power plants (VPPs) rely on static aggregation and plan based resource allocation strategies. These methods overlook differences in device response times and limit flexibility for ancillary services. To address this issue, we propose a dynamic virtual power plant (DVPP) that coordinates heterogeneous resources across multiple time scales using grid forming control. We first contrast grid following and grid forming converters: grid following designs rely on a phase locked loop which can undermine stability in weak grids, whereas our DVPP applies virtual synchronous generator control at the aggregate level to provide effective inertia and damping. Then, we introduce a dynamic participation factor framework that measures each device s contribution through the frequency active power and voltage reactive power loops. Exploiting device heterogeneity, we adopt a banded allocation strategy: slow resources manage steady state and low frequency regulation; intermediate resources smooth transitions; and fast resources deliver rapid response and high frequency damping. Comparative simulations demonstrate that this coordinated, timescale aware approach enhances stability and ancillary service performance compared to conventional VPPs.

AI.tldr: Proposes a dynamic virtual power plant (DVPP) coordinating heterogeneous DERs across multiple timescales using grid-forming control and banded resource allocation to enhance grid stability and ancillary services.
AI.motivation: Increasing DER penetration reduces traditional frequency/voltage support from synchronous machines, weakening grid stability and requiring fast adaptive control, especially in weak grids. Conventional VPPs use static aggregation that overlooks device response time differences and limits flexibility for ancillary services.
AI.method: DVPP coordinates heterogeneous resources across multiple timescales using grid-forming control (virtual synchronous generator at aggregate level). Introduces dynamic participation factor framework measuring device contributions through frequency-active power and voltage-reactive power loops. Uses banded allocation strategy: slow resources for steady-state/low-frequency regulation, intermediate for transition smoothing, fast for rapid response/high-frequency damping.
AI.result: Comparative simulations demonstrate the coordinated, timescale-aware approach enhances stability and ancillary service performance compared to conventional VPPs.
AI.conclusion: The DVPP framework effectively addresses limitations of static VPPs by leveraging device heterogeneity and grid-forming control to provide superior frequency/voltage support and stability in weak grids with high DER penetration.

title: Focus Then Listen: Exploring Plug-and-Play Audio Enhancer for Noise-Robust Large Audio Language Models
summary: Large audio language models (LALMs) are a class of foundation models for audio understanding. Existing LALMs tend to degrade significantly in real-world noisy acoustic conditions where speech and non-speech sounds interfere. While noise-aware fine-tuning can improve robustness, it requires task-specific noisy data and expensive retraining, limiting scalability. To address this issue, we propose Focus-Then-Listen (FTL), a plug-and-play audio enhancer that improves LALMs' noise robustness. Specifically, FTL first separates the input waveform into speech and non-speech, and a modality router is applied to predict the target audio modality (e.g., speech) based on the user's instruction. Finally, a modality-aware fusion block generates a task-adaptive enhanced signal for improved downstream perception and reasoning. Experiments across multiple LALMs and tasks show that FTL improves performance across different noise levels without fine-tuning on LALMs.

AI.tldr: FTL is a plug-and-play audio enhancer that improves noise robustness of Large Audio Language Models by separating speech/non-speech, routing target modality based on instructions, and generating task-adaptive enhanced signals.
AI.motivation: Existing Large Audio Language Models degrade significantly in real-world noisy conditions where speech and non-speech sounds interfere. While noise-aware fine-tuning can help, it requires task-specific noisy data and expensive retraining, limiting scalability.
AI.method: FTL (Focus-Then-Listen) is a plug-and-play audio enhancer that: 1) separates input waveform into speech and non-speech components, 2) applies a modality router to predict target audio modality based on user instructions, and 3) uses a modality-aware fusion block to generate task-adaptive enhanced signals for improved downstream perception and reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple LALMs and tasks show that FTL improves performance across different noise levels without requiring fine-tuning on the LALMs themselves.
AI.conclusion: FTL provides an effective plug-and-play solution for improving noise robustness in Large Audio Language Models, addressing the limitations of traditional noise-aware fine-tuning approaches that require task-specific data and expensive retraining.

title: A Unified Hybrid Control Architecture for Multi-DOF Robotic Manipulators
summary: Multi-degree-of-freedom (DOF) robotic manipulators exhibit strongly nonlinear, high-dimensional, and coupled dynamics, posing significant challenges for controller design. To address these issues, this work proposes a unified hybrid control architecture that integrates model predictive control (MPC) with feedback regulation, together with a stability analysis of the proposed scheme. The proposed approach mitigates the optimization difficulty associated with high-dimensional nonlinear systems and enhances overall control performance. Furthermore, a hardware implementation scheme based on machine learning (ML) is proposed to achieve high computational efficiency while maintaining control accuracy. Finally, simulation and hardware experiments under external disturbances validate the proposed architecture, demonstrating its superior performance, hardware feasibility, and generalization capability for multi-DOF manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: Proposes hybrid MPC-feedback control for multi-DOF manipulators with ML-based hardware implementation for computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Multi-DOF robotic manipulators have strongly nonlinear, high-dimensional, coupled dynamics that make controller design challenging. Existing approaches struggle with optimization difficulties in high-dimensional nonlinear systems and computational efficiency in hardware implementation.
AI.method: Unified hybrid control architecture integrating model predictive control (MPC) with feedback regulation, plus stability analysis. ML-based hardware implementation scheme for computational efficiency while maintaining accuracy.
AI.result: Simulation and hardware experiments under external disturbances validate the architecture, demonstrating superior performance, hardware feasibility, and generalization capability for multi-DOF manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid MPC-feedback control with ML-based hardware implementation effectively addresses challenges in multi-DOF manipulator control, offering improved performance, computational efficiency, and practical feasibility.

title: The First Environmental Sound Deepfake Detection Challenge: Benchmarking Robustness, Evaluation, and Insights
summary: Recent progress in audio generation has made it increasingly easy to create highly realistic environmental soundscapes, which can be misused to produce deceptive content, such as fake alarms, gunshots, and crowd sounds, raising concerns for public safety and trust. While deepfake detection for speech and singing voice has been extensively studied, environmental sound deepfake detection (ESDD) remains underexplored. To advance ESDD, the first edition of the ESDD challenge was launched, attracting 97 registered teams and receiving 1,748 valid submissions. This paper presents the task formulation, dataset construction, evaluation protocols, baseline systems, and key insights from the challenge results. Furthermore, we analyze common architectural choices and training strategies among top-performing systems. Finally, we discuss potential future research directions for ESDD, outlining key opportunities and open problems to guide subsequent studies in this field.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the first Environmental Sound Deepfake Detection (ESDD) challenge, addressing the underexplored problem of detecting fake environmental sounds, presents task formulation, dataset, evaluation, baseline systems, and analyzes top-performing approaches.
AI.motivation: Environmental sound deepfakes pose public safety risks (fake alarms, gunshots, crowd sounds), but detection research lags behind speech/singing voice deepfake detection, necessitating focused research and community engagement through a challenge.
AI.method: Organized the first ESDD challenge with 97 teams and 1,748 submissions; established task formulation, dataset construction, evaluation protocols, baseline systems; analyzed top-performing architectures and training strategies.
AI.result: The challenge successfully engaged the community with substantial participation; analysis revealed common effective architectural choices and training strategies among top performers; identified key insights for ESDD advancement.
AI.conclusion: The ESDD challenge establishes a foundation for environmental sound deepfake detection research, provides benchmark dataset and evaluation framework, and outlines future research directions to address open problems in this emerging field.

title: Receding-Horizon Maximum-Likelihood Estimation of Neural-ODE Dynamics and Thresholds from Event Cameras
summary: Event cameras emit asynchronous brightness-change events where each pixel triggers an event when the last event exceeds a threshold, yielding a history-dependent measurement model. We address online maximum-likelihood identification of continuous-time dynamics from such streams. The latent state follows a Neural ODE and is mapped to predicted log-intensity through a differentiable state-to-image model. We model events with a history-dependent marked point process whose conditional intensity is a smooth surrogate of contrast-threshold triggering, treating the contrast threshold as an unknown parameter. The resulting log-likelihood consists of an event term and a compensator integral. We propose a receding-horizon estimator that performs a few gradient steps per update on a receding horizon window. For streaming evaluation, we store two scalars per pixel (last-event time and estimated log-intensity at that time) and approximate the compensator via Monte Carlo pixel subsampling. Synthetic experiments demonstrate joint recovery of dynamics parameters and the contrast threshold, and characterize accuracy--latency trade-offs with respect to the window length.

AI.tldr: Online maximum-likelihood identification of continuous-time dynamics from event camera streams using Neural ODEs and marked point processes with receding-horizon estimation.
AI.motivation: Event cameras produce asynchronous brightness-change events with history-dependent measurement models, requiring specialized methods for online identification of continuous-time dynamics from such event streams.
AI.method: Model latent state with Neural ODE, map to predicted log-intensity via differentiable state-to-image model. Events modeled as history-dependent marked point process with smooth surrogate conditional intensity. Use receding-horizon estimator with gradient steps on window. For streaming, store two scalars per pixel and approximate compensator via Monte Carlo pixel subsampling.
AI.result: Synthetic experiments demonstrate joint recovery of dynamics parameters and contrast threshold, and characterize accuracy-latency trade-offs with respect to window length.
AI.conclusion: Proposed method enables online maximum-likelihood identification of continuous-time dynamics from event camera streams with efficient streaming implementation and demonstrated parameter recovery capabilities.

title: Training Dynamics-Aware Multi-Factor Curriculum Learning for Target Speaker Extraction
summary: Target speaker extraction (TSE) aims to isolate a specific speaker's voice from multi-speaker mixtures. Despite strong benchmark results, real-world performance often degrades due to different interacting factors. Previous curriculum learning approaches for TSE typically address these factors separately, failing to capture their complex interactions and relying on predefined difficulty factors that may not align with actual model learning behavior. To address this challenge, we first propose a multi-factor curriculum learning strategy that jointly schedules SNR thresholds, speaker counts, overlap ratios, and synthetic/real proportions, enabling progressive learning from simple to complex scenarios. However, determining optimal scheduling without predefined assumptions remains challenging. We therefore introduce TSE-Datamap, a visualization framework that grounds curriculum design in observed training dynamics by tracking confidence and variability across training epochs. Our analysis reveals three characteristic data regions: (i) easy-to-learn examples where models consistently perform well, (ii) ambiguous examples where models oscillate between alternative predictions, and (iii) hard-to-learn examples where models persistently struggle. Guided by these data-driven insights, our methods improve extraction results over random sampling, with particularly strong gains in challenging multi-speaker scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-factor curriculum learning strategy for target speaker extraction that jointly schedules multiple difficulty factors, and proposes TSE-Datamap, a visualization framework that grounds curriculum design in observed training dynamics by analyzing confidence and variability patterns across training epochs.
AI.motivation: Real-world target speaker extraction performance degrades due to interacting factors, but previous curriculum learning approaches address factors separately, fail to capture complex interactions, and rely on predefined difficulty factors that may not align with actual model learning behavior.
AI.method: 1) Multi-factor curriculum learning strategy that jointly schedules SNR thresholds, speaker counts, overlap ratios, and synthetic/real proportions for progressive learning. 2) TSE-Datamap visualization framework that tracks confidence and variability across training epochs to identify three characteristic data regions: easy-to-learn, ambiguous, and hard-to-learn examples.
AI.result: The methods improve extraction results over random sampling, with particularly strong gains in challenging multi-speaker scenarios, by using data-driven insights from TSE-Datamap analysis to guide curriculum design.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-factor curriculum learning strategy combined with the TSE-Datamap visualization framework provides a data-driven approach to curriculum design that captures complex factor interactions and aligns with actual model learning behavior, leading to improved target speaker extraction performance.

title: Formal Entropy-Regularized Control of Stochastic Systems
summary: Analyzing and controlling system entropy is a powerful tool for regulating predictability of control systems. Applications benefiting from such approaches range from reinforcement learning and data security to human-robot collaboration. In continuous-state stochastic systems, accurate entropy analysis and control remains a challenge. In recent years, finite-state abstractions of continuous systems have enabled control synthesis with formal performance guarantees on objectives such as stage costs. However, these results do not extend to entropy-based performance measures. We solve this problem by first obtaining bounds on the entropy of system discretizations using traditional formal-abstractions results, and then obtaining an additional bound on the difference between the entropy of a continuous distribution and that of its discretization. The resulting theory enables formal entropy-aware controller synthesis that trades predictability against control performance while preserving formal guarantees for the original continuous system. More specifically, we focus on minimizing the linear combination of the KL divergence of the system trajectory distribution to uniform -- our system entropy metric -- and a generic cumulative cost. We note that the bound we derive on the difference between the KL divergence to uniform of a given continuous distribution and its discretization can also be relevant in more general information-theoretic contexts. A set of case studies illustrates the effectiveness of the method.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method for formal entropy-aware controller synthesis in continuous-state stochastic systems by bounding discretization errors in entropy analysis.
AI.motivation: Accurate entropy analysis and control in continuous-state stochastic systems remains challenging, and existing finite-state abstraction methods for control synthesis don't extend to entropy-based performance measures, despite applications in reinforcement learning, data security, and human-robot collaboration.
AI.method: Develops bounds on entropy of system discretizations using formal abstraction results, plus additional bound on difference between entropy of continuous distribution and its discretization. Enables controller synthesis minimizing linear combination of KL divergence to uniform (entropy metric) and generic cumulative cost.
AI.result: Theoretical framework enables formal entropy-aware controller synthesis that trades predictability against control performance while preserving formal guarantees for original continuous system. Derived bound on KL divergence difference also relevant for general information-theoretic contexts.
AI.conclusion: Case studies demonstrate effectiveness of the method for formal entropy-aware control in continuous stochastic systems, bridging gap between finite-state abstractions and entropy-based performance measures.

title: TW-Sound580K: A Regional Audio-Text Dataset with Verification-Guided Curation for Localized Audio-Language Modeling
summary: Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) typically struggle with localized dialectal prosody due to the scarcity of specialized corpora. We present TW-Sound580K, a Taiwanese audio-text instruction dataset developed through a Verify-Generate-Critique (VGC) protocol. This pipeline leverages Dual-ASR validation to filter 522K raw clips, subsequently expanding them into 580,000 high-fidelity instruction pairs using a teacher model. The dataset's utility is demonstrated through Tai-LALM, which fine-tunes a DeSTA 2.5-Audio-initialized backbone and incorporates a dynamic Dual-ASR Arbitration strategy to optimize transcription selection during inference. On the TAU Benchmark, Tai-LALM reaches 49.1% accuracy, marking a 6.5% absolute improvement over the zero-shot baseline (42.6% with ASR text conditioning). This confirms that integrating regional corpora with rigorous curation and dynamic arbitration significantly enhances LALM performance on localized speech.

AI.tldr: TW-Sound580K dataset and Tai-LALM model improve Taiwanese dialectal prosody understanding in audio-language models through rigorous data curation and dynamic ASR arbitration.
AI.motivation: Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) typically struggle with localized dialectal prosody due to scarcity of specialized corpora, particularly for Taiwanese dialect.
AI.method: Developed TW-Sound580K dataset using Verify-Generate-Critique (VGC) protocol with Dual-ASR validation, then created Tai-LALM by fine-tuning DeSTA 2.5-Audio backbone with dynamic Dual-ASR Arbitration strategy for transcription selection during inference.
AI.result: Tai-LALM achieves 49.1% accuracy on TAU Benchmark, representing 6.5% absolute improvement over zero-shot baseline (42.6% with ASR text conditioning).
AI.conclusion: Integrating regional corpora with rigorous curation and dynamic arbitration significantly enhances LALM performance on localized speech, demonstrating the effectiveness of specialized datasets for dialectal prosody understanding.

title: Trajectory Tracking for Uncrewed Surface Vessels with Input Saturation and Dynamic Motion Constraints
summary: This work addresses the problem of constrained motion control of the uncrewed surface vessels. The constraints are imposed on states/inputs of the vehicles due to the physical limitations, mission requirements, and safety considerations. We develop a nonlinear feedback controller utilizing log-type Barrier Lyapunov Functions to enforce static and dynamic motion constraints. The proposed scheme uniquely addresses asymmetric constraints on position and heading alongside symmetric constraints on surge, sway, and yaw rates. Additionally, a smooth input saturation model is incorporated in the design to guarantee stability even under actuator bounds, which, if unaccounted for, can lead to severe performance degradation and poor tracking. Rigorous Lyapunov stability analysis shows that the closed-loop system remains stable and that all state variables remain within their prescribed bounds at all times, provided the initial conditions also lie within those bounds. Numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed strategies for surface vessels without violating the motion and actuator constraints.

AI.tldr: A nonlinear feedback controller using Barrier Lyapunov Functions for constrained motion control of uncrewed surface vessels, handling asymmetric position/heading constraints and symmetric velocity constraints while incorporating actuator saturation.
AI.motivation: Address constrained motion control of uncrewed surface vessels where constraints arise from physical limitations, mission requirements, and safety considerations. Unaccounted actuator bounds can lead to severe performance degradation and poor tracking.
AI.method: Develop a nonlinear feedback controller utilizing log-type Barrier Lyapunov Functions to enforce static and dynamic motion constraints. The scheme uniquely handles asymmetric constraints on position and heading alongside symmetric constraints on surge, sway, and yaw rates. Incorporates a smooth input saturation model to guarantee stability under actuator bounds.
AI.result: Rigorous Lyapunov stability analysis shows the closed-loop system remains stable with all state variables staying within prescribed bounds at all times (given initial conditions also lie within bounds). Numerical simulations demonstrate effectiveness without violating motion and actuator constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Barrier Lyapunov Function-based controller effectively addresses constrained motion control for uncrewed surface vessels, handling both state constraints and actuator saturation while maintaining stability and constraint satisfaction.

title: Boosting ASR Robustness via Test-Time Reinforcement Learning with Audio-Text Semantic Rewards
summary: Recently, Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems (e.g., Whisper) have achieved remarkable accuracy improvements but remain highly sensitive to real-world unseen data (data with large distribution shifts), including noisy environments and diverse accents. To address this issue, test-time adaptation (TTA) has shown great potential in improving the model adaptability at inference time without ground-truth labels, and existing TTA methods often rely on pseudo-labeling or entropy minimization. However, by treating model confidence as a learning signal, these methods may reinforce high-confidence errors, leading to confirmation bias that undermines adaptation. To overcome these limitations, we present ASR-TRA, a novel Test-time Reinforcement Adaptation framework inspired by causal intervention. More precisely, our method introduces a learnable decoder prompt and utilizes temperature-controlled stochastic decoding to generate diverse transcription candidates. These are scored by a reward model that measures audio-text semantic alignment, and the resulting feedback is used to update both model and prompt parameters via reinforcement learning. Comprehensive experiments on LibriSpeech with synthetic noise and L2 Arctic accented English datasets demonstrate that our method achieves higher accuracy while maintaining lower latency than existing TTA baselines. Ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of combining audio and language-based rewards, highlighting our method's enhanced stability and interpretability. Overall, our approach provides a practical and robust solution for deploying ASR systems in challenging real-world conditions.

AI.tldr: ASR-TRA: A test-time reinforcement adaptation framework for ASR systems that uses causal intervention, diverse transcription generation via temperature-controlled decoding, and audio-text semantic alignment rewards to improve robustness to noisy environments and diverse accents without ground-truth labels.
AI.motivation: Current ASR systems like Whisper achieve high accuracy but remain sensitive to real-world distribution shifts (noise, accents). Existing test-time adaptation methods rely on pseudo-labeling or entropy minimization, which can reinforce high-confidence errors through confirmation bias, undermining adaptation effectiveness.
AI.method: ASR-TRA introduces a learnable decoder prompt and uses temperature-controlled stochastic decoding to generate diverse transcription candidates. A reward model scores these candidates based on audio-text semantic alignment. Reinforcement learning updates both model and prompt parameters using this feedback, inspired by causal intervention principles.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on LibriSpeech with synthetic noise and L2 Arctic accented English datasets show ASR-TRA achieves higher accuracy while maintaining lower latency than existing TTA baselines. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of combining audio and language-based rewards, demonstrating enhanced stability and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: ASR-TRA provides a practical and robust solution for deploying ASR systems in challenging real-world conditions by overcoming confirmation bias in traditional TTA methods through reinforcement learning with semantic alignment rewards, offering improved adaptability to distribution shifts without ground-truth labels.

title: Uncertainty and Autarky: Cooperative Game Theory for Stable Local Energy Market Partitioning
summary: Local energy markets empower prosumers to form coalitions for energy trading. However, the optimal partitioning of the distribution grid into such coalitions remains unclear, especially in constrained grids with stochastic production and consumption. This analysis must take into account the interests of both the grid operator and the constituent prosumers. In this work, we present a cooperative game theoretic framework to study distribution grid partitioning into local energy market coalitions under uncertain prosumption and grid constraints. We formulate the optimal stable partitioning problem to balance the interests of the grid operator with that of prosumers. Under deterministic load and generation, we show that the largest market coalition is the optimal stable partition. For the case of stochastic loads and generation, we provide an algorithm to evaluate the optimal stable partition. Numerical experiments are performed on benchmark and real world distribution grids. Our results help in understanding how uncertainty affects local energy market partitioning decisions in constrained distribution grids.

AI.tldr: A cooperative game theory framework for partitioning distribution grids into local energy market coalitions under uncertainty, balancing grid operator and prosumer interests.
AI.motivation: Local energy markets enable prosumers to form coalitions for energy trading, but optimal partitioning of constrained distribution grids with stochastic production/consumption is unclear and must balance grid operator and prosumer interests.
AI.method: Cooperative game theoretic framework to study distribution grid partitioning into local energy market coalitions under uncertain prosumption and grid constraints; formulation of optimal stable partitioning problem; algorithm for evaluating optimal stable partition under stochastic loads/generation.
AI.result: Under deterministic conditions, largest market coalition is optimal stable partition; algorithm developed for stochastic case; numerical experiments on benchmark and real-world distribution grids show how uncertainty affects partitioning decisions.
AI.conclusion: The framework helps understand how uncertainty affects local energy market partitioning decisions in constrained distribution grids, providing insights for balancing grid operator and prosumer interests.

title: SLICE: Speech Enhancement via Layer-wise Injection of Conditioning Embeddings
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables, submitted to INTERSPEECH 2026
summary: Real-world speech is often corrupted by multiple degradations simultaneously, including additive noise, reverberation, and nonlinear distortion. Diffusion-based enhancement methods perform well on single degradations but struggle with compound corruptions. Prior noise-aware approaches inject conditioning at the input layer only, which can degrade performance below that of an unconditioned model. To address this, we propose injecting degradation conditioning, derived from a pretrained encoder with multi-task heads for noise type, reverberation, and distortion, into the timestep embedding so that it propagates through all residual blocks without architectural changes. In controlled experiments where only the injection method varies, input-level conditioning performs worse than no encoder at all on compound degradations, while layer-wise injection achieves the best results. The method also generalizes to diverse real-world recordings.

AI.tldr: Proposes layer-wise injection of degradation conditioning into timestep embeddings for diffusion-based speech enhancement, improving performance on compound corruptions compared to input-level conditioning.
AI.motivation: Real-world speech often suffers from multiple simultaneous degradations (noise, reverberation, distortion). Existing diffusion-based enhancement methods work well on single degradations but struggle with compound corruptions. Prior noise-aware approaches that inject conditioning only at the input layer can degrade performance below unconditioned models.
AI.method: Inject degradation conditioning derived from a pretrained encoder with multi-task heads (for noise type, reverberation, and distortion) into the timestep embedding. This allows conditioning to propagate through all residual blocks without architectural changes, unlike input-level conditioning.
AI.result: In controlled experiments, input-level conditioning performs worse than no encoder at all on compound degradations, while layer-wise injection achieves the best results. The method also generalizes well to diverse real-world recordings.
AI.conclusion: Layer-wise injection of degradation conditioning into timestep embeddings is an effective approach for diffusion-based speech enhancement on compound corruptions, outperforming input-level conditioning and maintaining generalization to real-world scenarios.

title: Computing Scaled Relative Graphs of Discrete-time LTI Systems from Data
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, submitted for possible publication
summary: Graphical methods for system analysis have played a central role in control theory. A recently emerging tool in this field is the Scaled Relative Graph (SRG). In this paper, we further extend its applicability by showing how the SRG of discrete-time linear-time-invariant (LTI) systems can be computed exactly from its state-space representation using linear matrix inequalities. We additionally propose a fully data-driven approach where we demonstrate how to compute the SRG exclusively from input-output data. Furthermore, we introduce a robust version of the SRG, which can be computed from noisy data trajectories and contains the SRG of the actual system.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Scaled Relative Graph (SRG) analysis for discrete-time LTI systems with exact computation from state-space models using LMIs, data-driven computation from input-output data, and robust SRG estimation from noisy data.
AI.motivation: Graphical methods are fundamental in control theory, and SRG is an emerging tool that needs broader applicability. The authors aim to extend SRG analysis to discrete-time LTI systems and develop both model-based and data-driven computation methods, including robust handling of noisy data.
AI.method: Three main contributions: 1) Exact SRG computation for discrete-time LTI systems from state-space representation using linear matrix inequalities (LMIs); 2) Fully data-driven approach computing SRG exclusively from input-output data without requiring system model; 3) Robust SRG formulation that can be computed from noisy data trajectories and provides guaranteed containment of the actual system's SRG.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates successful computation of SRG for discrete-time LTI systems through both model-based (LMI) and data-driven approaches. The robust SRG method provides reliable system characterization even with measurement noise, ensuring the computed SRG contains the true system's SRG.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods significantly extend SRG applicability to discrete-time LTI systems, offering both exact model-based computation and practical data-driven alternatives. The robust SRG formulation enables reliable system analysis from noisy experimental data, enhancing SRG's utility as a graphical analysis tool in control theory.

title: Latent-Mark: An Audio Watermark Robust to Neural Resynthesis
summary: While existing audio watermarking techniques have achieved strong robustness against traditional digital signal processing (DSP) attacks, they remain vulnerable to neural resynthesis. This occurs because modern neural audio codecs act as semantic filters and discard the imperceptible waveform variations used in prior watermarking methods. To address this limitation, we propose Latent-Mark, the first zero-bit audio watermarking framework designed to survive semantic compression. Our key insight is that robustness to the encode-decode process requires embedding the watermark within the codec's invariant latent space. We achieve this by optimizing the audio waveform to induce a detectable directional shift in its encoded latent representation, while constraining perturbations to align with the natural audio manifold to ensure imperceptibility. To prevent overfitting to a single codec's quantization rules, we introduce Cross-Codec Optimization, jointly optimizing the waveform across multiple surrogate codecs to target shared latent invariants. Extensive evaluations demonstrate robust zero-shot transferability to unseen neural codecs, achieving state-of-the-art resilience against traditional DSP attacks while preserving perceptual imperceptibility. Our work inspires future research into universal watermarking frameworks capable of maintaining integrity across increasingly complex and diverse generative distortions.

AI.tldr: Latent-Mark is a zero-bit audio watermarking framework designed to survive neural resynthesis by embedding watermarks in codec-invariant latent spaces, using cross-codec optimization for robustness across unseen neural codecs.
AI.motivation: Existing audio watermarking techniques are vulnerable to neural resynthesis because modern neural audio codecs act as semantic filters that discard the imperceptible waveform variations used in prior methods. There's a need for watermarking that can survive semantic compression.
AI.method: Latent-Mark embeds watermarks within codec's invariant latent space by optimizing audio waveforms to induce detectable directional shifts in encoded latent representations while constraining perturbations to align with the natural audio manifold for imperceptibility. Cross-Codec Optimization jointly optimizes waveforms across multiple surrogate codecs to target shared latent invariants, preventing overfitting to single codec quantization rules.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations demonstrate robust zero-shot transferability to unseen neural codecs, achieving state-of-the-art resilience against traditional DSP attacks while preserving perceptual imperceptibility.
AI.conclusion: Latent-Mark represents the first zero-bit audio watermarking framework designed to survive semantic compression, inspiring future research into universal watermarking frameworks capable of maintaining integrity across increasingly complex and diverse generative distortions.

title: A Comprehensive Approach to Directly Addressing Estimation Delays in Stochastic Guidance
comment: Submitted to journal publication. 46 pages, 12 figures
summary: In realistic pursuit-evasion scenarios, abrupt target maneuvers generate unavoidable periods of elevated uncertainty that result in estimation delays. Such delays can degrade interception performance to the point of causing a miss. Existing delayed-information guidance laws fail to provide a complete remedy, as they typically assume constant and known delays. Moreover, in practice they are fed by filtered estimates, contrary to these laws' foundational assumptions. We present an overarching strategy for tracking and interception that explicitly accounts for time-varying estimation delays. We first devise a guidance law that incorporates two time-varying delays, thereby generalizing prior deterministic formulations. This law is driven by a particle-based fixed-lag smoother that provides it with appropriately delayed state estimates. Furthermore, using semi-Markov modeling of the target's maneuvers, the delays are estimated in real-time, enabling adaptive adjustment of the guidance inputs during engagement. The resulting framework consistently conjoins estimation, delay modeling, and guidance. Its effectiveness and superior robustness over existing delayed-information guidance laws are demonstrated via an extensive Monte Carlo study.

AI.tldr: Novel guidance framework addresses time-varying estimation delays in pursuit-evasion scenarios using particle-based smoothing and semi-Markov maneuver modeling for adaptive guidance.
AI.motivation: Existing delayed-information guidance laws assume constant known delays and use filtered estimates, failing to handle realistic time-varying estimation delays caused by abrupt target maneuvers, which degrade interception performance.
AI.method: Developed guidance law with two time-varying delays, particle-based fixed-lag smoother for delayed state estimates, and semi-Markov modeling of target maneuvers for real-time delay estimation and adaptive guidance adjustment.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates superior effectiveness and robustness over existing delayed-information guidance laws through extensive Monte Carlo studies.
AI.conclusion: Proposed framework successfully integrates estimation, delay modeling, and guidance to address time-varying estimation delays in realistic pursuit-evasion scenarios, overcoming limitations of existing approaches.

title: Building Enterprise Realtime Voice Agents from Scratch: A Technical Tutorial
summary: We present a technical tutorial for building enterprise-grade realtime voice agents from first principles. While over 25 open-source speech-to-speech models and numerous voice agent frameworks exist, no single resource explains the complete pipeline from individual components to a working streaming voice agent with function calling capabilities. Through systematic investigation, we find that (1) native speech-to-speech models like Qwen2.5-Omni, while capable of high-quality audio generation, are too slow for realtime interaction ($\sim$13s time-to-first-audio); (2) the industry-standard approach uses a cascaded streaming pipeline: STT $\rightarrow$ LLM $\rightarrow$ TTS, where each component streams its output to the next; and (3) the key to ``realtime'' is not any single fast model but rather \textit{streaming and pipelining} across components. We build a complete voice agent using Deepgram (streaming STT), vLLM-served LLMs with function calling (streaming text generation), and ElevenLabs (streaming TTS), achieving a measured P50 time-to-first-audio of 947ms (best case 729ms) with cloud LLM APIs, and comparable latency with self-hosted vLLM on NVIDIA A10G GPU. We release the full codebase as a tutorial with working, tested code for every component.

AI.tldr: A tutorial for building realtime voice agents using a cascaded streaming pipeline (STT → LLM → TTS) that achieves sub-second latency through streaming and pipelining, outperforming native speech-to-speech models.
AI.motivation: Despite numerous open-source speech-to-speech models and voice agent frameworks, there's no comprehensive resource explaining the complete pipeline for building enterprise-grade realtime voice agents with function calling capabilities from first principles.
AI.method: Systematic investigation reveals that native speech-to-speech models are too slow for realtime use. The solution is a cascaded streaming pipeline: Deepgram (streaming STT) → vLLM-served LLMs with function calling (streaming text generation) → ElevenLabs (streaming TTS), with streaming and pipelining across components.
AI.result: Achieved P50 time-to-first-audio of 947ms (best case 729ms) with cloud LLM APIs, and comparable latency with self-hosted vLLM on NVIDIA A10G GPU, significantly faster than native speech-to-speech models (~13s).
AI.conclusion: The key to realtime voice agents is not any single fast model but rather streaming and pipelining across components. The tutorial provides complete, tested code for building enterprise-grade realtime voice agents with function calling.
